text: Ready? Don't hold back.
Fence.
Most parents know the feeling when your kids team up against you and it's two against one.
But Greg Masylas wants it that way. His kids don't just call him dad, they also call him coach.
I have a lot of fencers say the one thing, you know, you've proven something that was always told that you can never teach your children to fence.
If you ask 18-year-old Alexander and 15-year-old Sabrina, he didn't really have a choice.
I'd be crying here sometimes and he'd be like, no, you have to wait until you're seven.
I had this rule about second grade, that they have to start in second grade.
Like it's in our blood or something.
Sabrina is a freshman at the Drew School in San Francisco and Alexander is a senior.
They admit it's hard not to get a little competitive.
Here and there we'll get on each other's case.
In the fencing world, they are practically one.
Alex and Sabrina are both champion fencers.
Greg is a three-time Olympian and the coach of the U.S. Olympic team.
Those competitions are like, oh yeah, Masylas, like the fencing family with Alex and your dad.
Don't hesitate, Sabrina. Go for it.
When one of his kids is competing, chances are you will see Greg on the sidelines.
I think a lot of people would hate it having their father as their coach.
And many people ask me, but personally I think that it benefits me.
On the strip, he's my coach. At home, he's my dad.
So, you know, there's really a separation there.
Of course if they win, if he doesn't do well, then I have to kind of like comfort him a little bit as a father
rather than as the coach, sort of, you know, have to be a little bit more stoic and look at the things that he didn't do that were quite right.
Once we go home, it's perfectly normal.
It's just like we're not even fencers anymore.
It may not be a conventional family dynamic, but it's paid off.
Alex will compete in the 2012 Olympic Games as the youngest member of the U.S. men's fencing team.
And on July 27th, he will walk in the opening ceremonies with his dad by his side.
I'm really looking forward to being able to walk in the opening ceremonies hand in hand and really experience that moment.
That's going to be so much fun.
That's something rather special.
I love to follow in the footsteps of my father, but hopefully take his dream a step further as well.
You know, coming home with a medal, especially a gold one.
His dreams are coming true.
It's just, it's interesting to see when your dreams start to become a reality.
